name: Mathis
short_description: Explore the flat coastal plains of South Texas near Mathis for agate and chalcedony specimens. This easily accessible rockhounding area offers productive collecting with minimal effort.
description:
The Mathis area in San Patricio County sits within South Texas's productive coastal plain geology, where weathering and erosion have exposed silica-rich deposits ideal for agate and chalcedony hunting. The region's flat, open terrain and sparse vegetation make it easy to scan wide areas for specimens. This location is particularly appealing to beginners and families seeking an accessible rockhounding experience without steep terrain or difficult access. The area experiences minimal elevation change, making it suitable for collectors of all mobility levels. Collectors should bring plenty of water and sun protection, as the open prairie offers little shade and summer heat can be intense.

The geological setting here reflects the Gulf Coastal Plain's depositional history, where ancient marine and fluvial sediments have been weathered to expose secondary mineral formations. Agate nodules and chalcedony specimens can be found in exposed cuts, wash areas, and scattered across the surface where natural erosion has done much of the work. The best finds typically occur in areas with recent drainage activity or road cuts that expose fresh material. Success here often depends on careful surface prospecting and knowing where recent rains or maintenance have exposed new ground.
history: The Mathis area sits within the Gulf Coastal Plain, where Quaternary and Tertiary sediments have been shaped by weathering and fluvial processes. Silica-rich deposits characteristic of South Texas formed through millions of years of marine and continental sedimentation, with agate and chalcedony nodules concentrated in weathered outcrops and ancient stream deposits.
tips: Search along dry creek beds, erosion gullies, and recent road cuts where fresh material is exposed and lighter-colored agate and chalcedony nodules stand out against darker soils. Bring a small cracking hammer to test suspect rocks and expose fresh surfaces - many nodules are disguised by weathered exterior coatings. Visit after heavy rains when wash areas have been freshly cut and new material is visible; peak collecting often occurs within a week of significant precipitation. Focus on areas with visible chalky or tan-colored soil patches, which indicate silica-rich zones where nodules concentrate.
terrain_notes: Flat prairie terrain at 28 feet elevation with minimal slope and scattered low vegetation typical of South Texas coastal plains. Walking distance from parking is variable depending on site access, but terrain is generally easy to navigate with firm ground and no major obstacles. Footing is relatively stable on packed soil and caliche, though wet conditions after rain can make some areas muddy.
best_season: October - April. South Texas coastal region experiences intense summer heat (often exceeding 95°F) and afternoon thunderstorms in summer months. Fall and winter offer cooler temperatures, lower humidity, and improved visibility. Spring collecting is also viable but heat increases by April.
written_directions: From La Fruta, take TX 77 south toward Mathis for approximately 8 miles. Look for accessible ranch land, county roads, or obvious erosion washes on the east side of TX 77 where agate and chalcedony specimens are visible from the road. Coordinate with local landowners before accessing private property. Alternative access may be available via county roads branching east from TX 77 in the Mathis vicinity - scout these routes for pullouts and exposed material.
road_conditions: County roads and ranch access roads are typically well-maintained but unpaved or lightly paved. Standard passenger vehicles can navigate most routes in dry conditions; avoid wet conditions when caliche roads become slippery.
parking_notes: Parking depends on specific access point and landowner permission. Most productive areas are accessed via county road pullouts or ranch entrances. No established parking lot; plan to pull well off the road and ask permission from nearby residences.
collection_rules: Private land rockhounding is permitted only with explicit written permission from the landowner. Do not trespass or cross fences without authorization. Collect agate, chalcedony, and quartz specimens for personal use only. Leave all equipment gates open as found and pack out all trash.
rules:
Obtain landowner permission before accessing private property
Do not trespass across fenced areas
Personal use collection only - no commercial activity
Pack out all trash and leave site undisturbed
Close gates behind you if encountered
Respect posted no-trespassing signs
nearest_services: Mathis, Texas, approximately 8 miles south; offers gas, food, water, and supplies. La Fruta is the nearest immediate town with limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and collectors with moderate mobility. No steep slopes or significant elevation changes. Ground surface is firm packed soil and caliche, generally easy to walk on. Unpaved county roads to access areas may be rough but passable by standard vehicles in dry conditions. Not wheelchair accessible but manageable for most able-bodied collectors.

faq.question: What types of agates and chalcedony can you find rockhounding near Mathis, Texas?
faq.answer: The Mathis area is known for banded agate, chalcedony nodules, and colorless to milky quartz specimens. Most finds are tan, gray, or white chalcedony with occasional specimens showing banding or color variation. Pieces typically range from 1 to 4 inches but larger specimens are possible in wash areas.

faq.question: Do you need a permit or pay a fee to rockhound at Mathis, Texas?
faq.answer: No permit is required, but the Mathis area is primarily on private land. You must obtain explicit permission from the landowner before collecting. There are no established fee dig sites at this location - all collecting requires private property access and authorization.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding at Mathis and why?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for Mathis rockhounding. South Texas experiences extreme summer heat exceeding 95°F and afternoon thunderstorms, making fall and winter months more comfortable for field work. Spring is viable but temperatures rise quickly by late April. Collecting after heavy rains can be particularly productive when wash areas expose fresh material.

faq.question: How difficult is it to rockhound at Mathis for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Mathis is excellent for beginners and families due to its flat terrain, easy access, and abundant surface material. No special skills or experience are required - specimens are often visible on the surface and the landscape is simple to navigate. Children ages 6 and up can participate comfortably, and the area requires minimal physical exertion.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should you bring for rockhounding near Mathis, Texas?
faq.answer: Bring a small cracking hammer to test rocks and expose fresh surfaces, since many nodules are disguised by weathered coatings. Also pack a 5-gallon bucket for specimens, a hand lens for inspecting material, sturdy shoes or boots, plenty of water (at least 2-3 gallons per person), sunscreen, and a hat. A small trowel is helpful for digging specimens out of soil.

faq.question: Where exactly should you look for agate and chalcedony around Mathis?
faq.answer: Focus on dry creek beds, erosion gullies, and road cuts where fresh material is exposed and specimens are visible against darker soil. Areas with tan or chalky-colored soil patches indicate silica-rich zones where nodules concentrate. After significant rainfall, newly-cut washes often expose the best finds within a week of the rain event.

meta_description: Hunt agate, chalcedony, and quartz near Mathis, Texas on private land. Flat terrain, easy collecting for beginners. Permission required. Best October-April.
